Adversarially review a generated LLD session. Be a skeptic — find REAL defects, do not rubber-stamp. Review-only: DO NOT modify files.

Workspace: /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/9a6dfee3-23d7-4977-ba89-3f9513c33122_lld/  (source files + _response.md)
Run tests yourself: `cd /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/9a6dfee3-23d7-4977-ba89-3f9513c33122_lld && /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python main.py` and `/Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python -m pytest -q`.

Problem: Design a Message Broker (Pub-Sub). Required distinct concurrency angle: CONDITION-VARIABLE wait/notify + PER-CONSUMER-GROUP OFFSETS: each topic holds an append-only log; each consumer group tracks its own read offset; a poll() blocks on a threading.Condition until publish() appends and notifies. Observer pattern (subscribers), per-topic lock. The concurrency test: producers publish N messages while M consumer-group threads poll concurrently (Barrier-synced); assert every group reads ALL N messages exactly once in order, offsets end at N, and no message lost or duplicated.

Report PASS/FAIL with evidence (file:line) on each:
1. CONCURRENCY CORRECTNESS: is the claimed angle actually implemented and correct? Can the invariant be violated (lost update, lost/duplicated message, double-dispense, negative stock, miscounted TTL)? Any deadlock, lost-wakeup (Condition not re-checked in a while loop / notify missed), or lock/permit leak? Does release/notify happen even when a callback or factory raises a NON-domain exception? Is slow I/O kept outside the lock?
2. CONCURRENCY TEST STRENGTH: does the test PROVE the invariant under real contention (Barrier + strict post-state: counts AND uniqueness AND typed losers AND committed-state)? Or could it pass trivially (work serialises, peak never reaches the limit, no hold in the critical section)? For scheduler/TTL: is the injected Clock used instead of real sleeps?
3. §3.5 API section present with a real typed contract table (Method | Signature | Returns | Raises | Purpose)? Every method also in §4.1/§4.2/§6?
4. §4.1: does EVERY class subsection end with a COMPLETE fenced class block after its (c) table (a method-less record still needs its fields block)?
5. Does the §6 ```python code match the actual disk files (no drift, no '...' bodies)?
6. EARN YOUR PATTERNS: are all claimed patterns genuinely realized in code (real State objects/transitions, real Strategy interface+impls, real Observer registration, real read-write lock)?
7. MERMAID: exactly 2 blocks; NO semicolon ';' inside any sequenceDiagram message text; both would parse in a real renderer.
8. Python: 3.11+ idioms only, no bare except, one domain exception base, no dead code.

Return verdict SHIP or FIX-FIRST and a findings list (severity/where/problem/fix). Only BLOCKER/MAJOR block; list MINORs too.

1	# Python 3.11+
2	"""
3	models.py — the "nouns" of the message broker.
4	
5	This file holds the domain entities, the one base exception, and its specific
6	subclasses. The two interesting entities are:
7	
8	  - Message:       an immutable record that lives forever in a topic's log.
9	  - Topic:         an append-only log PLUS a threading.Condition. This is the
10	                   heart of the wait/notify design — publish() appends and
11	                   wakes any consumer blocked in poll().
12	  - ConsumerGroup: each group remembers its OWN read offset into the log, so
13	                   every group sees every message independently.
14	
15	WHY a separate ConsumerGroup that just holds an int offset?  Because the offset
16	is RELATIONAL state — it describes "where THIS group has read up to in THIS
17	topic", not anything physical about the message. Keeping it on its own object
18	lets each group advance independently without touching the shared log.
19	"""
20	
21	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
22	from threading import Condition
23	
24	
25	# ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
26	# One base exception for the whole broker so callers can `except BrokerError`
27	# and catch every failure mode this system can raise, while still being able to
28	# catch a specific subclass when they care about the exact reason.
29	# 🎙️ "Every error my broker raises is a BrokerError. That means a caller can
30	#     catch the whole family in one line, or one specific reason if they want."
31	class BrokerError(Exception):
32	    """Base class for every error the broker raises."""
33	
34	
35	class UnknownTopicError(BrokerError):
36	    """Raised when a caller names a topic that was never created."""
37	
38	
39	class DuplicateTopicError(BrokerError):
40	    """Raised when create_topic is called twice with the same name."""
41	
42	
43	class UnknownConsumerGroupError(BrokerError):
44	    """Raised when a caller names a consumer group that never subscribed."""
45	
46	
47	class EmptyPayloadError(BrokerError):
48	    """Raised when a producer tries to publish a None/empty payload."""
49	
50	
51	class PollTimeoutError(BrokerError):
52	    """Raised when poll() waited the full timeout and no message arrived."""
53	
54	
55	# ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
56	# A Message never changes once published — it is frozen. Its offset is its
57	# permanent position in the topic's log (0, 1, 2, ...). That offset is how a
58	# consumer group remembers "I have read up to here".
59	# 🎙️ "A message is immutable. Once it's in the log at offset 7, it stays at
60	#     offset 7 forever — that's what lets every group read it independently."
61	@dataclass(frozen=True)
62	class Message:
63	    """One published message. Frozen — nothing mutates it after publish."""
64	
65	    offset: int          # permanent position in the topic log (0-based)
66	    topic: str           # which topic this belongs to
67	    payload: str         # the actual content the producer sent
68	    timestamp: float     # when it was appended (from the injected Clock)
69	
70	
71	# ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
72	# A Topic is an append-only log of Messages plus the Condition variable that
73	# producers and consumers coordinate on. The Condition is the ONE object both
74	# sides share: publish() appends then notify_all(); poll() wait()s on it until
75	# woken. Bundling the log and its Condition on one object keeps the locking
76	# scoped per-topic — two different topics never block each other.
77	# 🎙️ "Each topic is its own append-only log with its own condition variable.
78	#     A publish appends and wakes the sleepers; a poll sleeps until woken.
79	#     Per-topic, so unrelated topics never contend."
80	@dataclass
81	class Topic:
82	    """An append-only log for one topic, plus the condition consumers wait on."""
83	
84	    name: str
85	    # WHY: a plain list IS the log. Index == offset, append is O(1) amortised,
86	    #      and a consumer at offset k reads log[k] in O(1). No fancier structure
87	    #      earns its keep at interview scale.
88	    log: list[Message] = field(default_factory=list)
89	    # WHY: the Condition owns its OWN lock. Everything that touches `log` (and
90	    #      the offsets of groups on this topic) happens while holding it, and
91	    #      poll() releases it automatically while it sleeps inside cond.wait().
92	    cond: Condition = field(default_factory=Condition)
93	
94	    def next_offset(self) -> int:
95	        """The offset the NEXT appended message will get == current length."""
96	        # WHY: offsets are dense and 0-based, so the length of the log is
97	        #      exactly the position of the next message. No separate counter to
98	        #      drift out of sync.
99	        return len(self.log)
100	
101	
102	# ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
103	# A ConsumerGroup is the cursor. It remembers, per topic, how far this group
104	# has read. Two groups subscribed to the same topic each carry their own
105	# cursor, which is THE reason every group sees every message independently.
106	# 🎙️ "A consumer group is just a named cursor. It remembers its committed
107	#     offset per topic. Group A at offset 3 and group B at offset 0 read the
108	#     same log totally independently."
109	@dataclass
110	class ConsumerGroup:
111	    """A named subscriber that tracks its own committed read offset per topic."""
112	
113	    name: str
114	    # WHY: offset PER topic — one group can subscribe to many topics and must
115	    #      track each independently. dict[topic_name -> committed offset].
116	    offsets: dict[str, int] = field(default_factory=dict)
117	
118	    def committed_offset(self, topic_name: str) -> int:
119	        """How far this group has read in the given topic (0 if brand new)."""
120	        return self.offsets.get(topic_name, 0)
121

1	# Python 3.11+
2	"""
3	broker_service.py — the orchestrator.
4	
5	MessageBroker is the brain. It owns the topics and the consumer groups, and it
6	implements the four public operations: create_topic, subscribe, publish, poll.
7	
8	THE ONE SMART INSIGHT for this problem
9	--------------------------------------
10	A topic is an append-only LOG, and each consumer group is just a CURSOR (an
11	integer offset) into that log. Reading is "give me log[my_offset], then bump my
12	offset". Because the log never deletes and each group keeps its own cursor,
13	every group sees every message exactly once, in order, independently — there is
14	no per-consumer copy of the data and no fan-out queue to keep in sync.
15	
16	The blocking read is a textbook condition-variable wait/notify:
17	  - poll() holds the topic's lock, and WHILE there is nothing new at my offset,
18	    it cond.wait()s — which atomically releases the lock and sleeps.
19	  - publish() holds the same lock, appends, then cond.notify_all() — which wakes
20	    every sleeping consumer to re-check.
21	
22	CONCURRENCY MODEL
23	-----------------
24	The CLEAN core in this file is written as if single-threaded — there is exactly
25	ONE concurrency seam, the `_topic_cv(topic)` hook, which hands back the object a
26	poll() waits on and a publish() notifies on. In the clean core that hook just
27	returns the topic's own Condition, and ALL the wait/notify logic is already
28	real (it has to be — blocking IS the feature here). The §9 ThreadSafeMessageBroker
29	subclass adds a SECOND guard: a lock around the topic/group REGISTRIES so two
30	threads creating a topic or subscribing at the same instant can't corrupt the
31	dicts. The hot path (publish/poll) is already correct via the per-topic
32	Condition's own lock; the subclass only hardens the registry mutations.
33	"""
34	
35	from collections.abc import Iterator
36	from contextlib import contextmanager
37	from threading import Lock
38	
39	from gateways import Clock, SystemClock
40	from models import (
41	    ConsumerGroup,
42	    DuplicateTopicError,
43	    EmptyPayloadError,
44	    Message,
45	    PollTimeoutError,
46	    Topic,
47	    UnknownConsumerGroupError,
48	    UnknownTopicError,
49	)
50	
51	
52	# ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
53	# The single coordinator. It is the only thing that sees ALL topics and ALL
54	# groups, so by Information Expert it owns topic creation, subscription, and the
55	# publish/poll routing. The entities (Topic, ConsumerGroup) own the small bits
56	# of state intrinsic to them (the log, the offset); the broker owns the
57	# relationships between them.
58	# 🎙️ "The broker is the brain. It holds the topics and the groups and wires a
59	#     publish to the right log and a poll to the right cursor. The clever part
60	#     is how little it does — the log and the cursor do the real work."
61	class MessageBroker:
62	    """In-memory topic-based pub/sub broker (clean single-threaded core)."""
63	
64	    def __init__(self, clock: Clock | None = None) -> None:
65	        # WHY: dict by name for O(1) routing of a publish/poll/subscribe to the
66	        #      right topic. Topic creation is explicit, so an unknown name is a
67	        #      clear typed error rather than a silent auto-create.
68	        self._topics: dict[str, Topic] = {}
69	        # WHY: groups live here, keyed by name, so subscribe() is idempotent-ish
70	        #      lookups and poll() can fetch the cursor object in O(1).
71	        self._groups: dict[str, ConsumerGroup] = {}
72	        # WHY: inject the clock so message timestamps are deterministic in tests.
73	        #      A message is identified by (topic, offset) — the offset IS the id,
74	        #      so the broker mints no separate ids.
75	        self._clock: Clock = clock or SystemClock()
76	
77	    # ── concurrency seam #1: the wait/notify object for a topic ──────────────
78	    # WHY: poll() must wait on, and publish() must notify, the SAME object. We
79	    #      funnel both through this one hook so there is a single, named place
80	    #      that decides "what do I block on for this topic". In the clean core
81	    #      it is simply the topic's own Condition. The blocking logic itself is
82	    #      already real — it has to be, because blocking IS the feature.
83	    def _topic_cv(self, topic: Topic):
84	        return topic.cond
85	
86	    # ── concurrency seam #2: a no-op guard around REGISTRY mutations ─────────
87	    # WHY: in the clean single-threaded core, creating a topic or subscribing a
88	    #      group needs no lock — nobody else is mutating the dicts. This hook
89	    #      does nothing. The thread-safe subclass overrides ONLY this to wrap a
90	    #      real lock around the dict mutations, so two threads can't corrupt the
91	    #      registry while adding a key at the same instant.
92	    # 🎙️ "My core has zero registry-lock code. The thread-safe version overrides
93	    #     this one hook with a real lock. The diff is a few lines."
94	    @contextmanager
95	    def _registry_guard(self) -> Iterator[None]:
96	        yield  # does nothing — the thread-safe subclass overrides this
97	
98	    # ── public API ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
99	
100	    def create_topic(self, name: str) -> None:
101	        """Create a new, empty topic. Raises if the name is already taken."""
102	        # WHY: validate the boundary first — empty name is a programming error,
103	        #      so reject it with a typed error before mutating anything.
104	        if not name:
105	            raise EmptyPayloadError("topic name must be non-empty")
106	
107	        with self._registry_guard():
108	            # WHY: re-check existence INSIDE the guard so two concurrent
109	            #      create_topic calls can't both pass the check and both insert.
110	            if name in self._topics:
111	                raise DuplicateTopicError(f"topic already exists: {name}")
112	            self._topics[name] = Topic(name=name)
113	
114	    def subscribe(self, group_name: str, topic_name: str) -> ConsumerGroup:
115	        """Subscribe a consumer group to a topic; returns the group cursor.
116	
117	        A brand-new group starts reading at offset 0 (the beginning of the log),
118	        so it sees every message ever published to the topic. Subscribing the
119	        same group to the same topic twice is safe — it keeps its existing
120	        offset and does not rewind.
121	        """
122	        if not group_name:
123	            raise EmptyPayloadError("group name must be non-empty")
124	
125	        topic = self._require_topic(topic_name)
126	
127	        with self._registry_guard():
128	            # WHY: get-or-create the group inside the guard so two threads
129	            #      subscribing the same group name don't create two cursors.
130	            group = self._groups.get(group_name)
131	
132	            if group is None:
133	                group = ConsumerGroup(name=group_name)
134	                self._groups[group_name] = group
135	
136	            # WHY: setdefault, NOT plain assignment — re-subscribing must NOT
137	            #      reset a group that has already read some messages back to 0.
138	            #      That would make it re-read everything (duplicates).
139	            group.offsets.setdefault(topic.name, 0)
140	
141	        return group
142	
143	    def publish(self, topic_name: str, payload: str) -> Message:
144	        """Append a message to a topic's log and wake all blocked consumers."""
145	        # WHY: reject an empty payload at the boundary — a None/empty publish is
146	        #      almost always a bug, and silently appending it would let blocked
147	        #      consumers wake on garbage.
148	        if not payload:
149	            raise EmptyPayloadError("cannot publish an empty payload")
150	
151	        topic = self._require_topic(topic_name)
152	        cv = self._topic_cv(topic)
153	
154	        # WHY: hold the topic's condition lock for the append. This is the
155	        #      critical section: compute the offset and append as one atomic
156	        #      step so two publishers can't both grab the same offset.
157	        with cv:
158	            message = Message(
159	                offset=topic.next_offset(),
160	                topic=topic.name,
161	                payload=payload,
162	                timestamp=self._clock.now(),
163	            )
164	            topic.log.append(message)
165	            # WHY: notify_all (not notify) — several consumer GROUPS may be
166	            #      blocked on this topic, and every one of them needs to wake to
167	            #      check whether this new message is at ITS offset.
168	            cv.notify_all()
169	
170	        return message
171	
172	    def poll(self, group_name: str, topic_name: str, timeout: float | None = None) -> Message:
173	        """Read the next message for a group, BLOCKING until one is available.
174	
175	        Returns the message at the group's committed offset and advances the
176	        offset by one (so the next poll returns the following message). If no
177	        message is available it blocks on the topic's condition until a publish
178	        wakes it. If `timeout` is given and elapses first, raises PollTimeoutError.
179	        """
180	        topic = self._require_topic(topic_name)
181	        group = self._require_group(group_name)
182	        cv = self._topic_cv(topic)
183	
184	        with cv:
185	            # WHY: wait_for re-checks the predicate every time it is woken and
186	            #      handles spurious wakeups for free. The predicate is "there is
187	            #      a message at MY committed offset", i.e. the log is longer than
188	            #      where I've read up to. It returns False only on timeout.
189	            ready = cv.wait_for(
190	                lambda: topic.next_offset() > group.committed_offset(topic.name),
191	                timeout=timeout,
192	            )
193	
194	            if not ready:
195	                raise PollTimeoutError(
196	                    f"no message for group {group_name!r} on topic {topic_name!r} "
197	                    f"within {timeout}s"
198	                )
199	
200	            # WHY: read at my cursor, then bump my cursor by one — this is the
201	            #      "commit". Holding the lock makes read-then-commit atomic, so a
202	            #      message is delivered to this group exactly once even if many
203	            #      of this group's threads poll at the same instant.
204	            offset = group.committed_offset(topic.name)
205	            message = topic.log[offset]
206	            group.offsets[topic.name] = offset + 1
207	
208	        return message
209	
210	    # ── read-only helpers (used by main.py and tests) ────────────────────────
211	
212	    def topic_size(self, topic_name: str) -> int:
213	        """How many messages have been published to a topic (== next offset)."""
214	        return self._require_topic(topic_name).next_offset()
215	
216	    def committed_offset(self, group_name: str, topic_name: str) -> int:
217	        """How far a group has read in a topic — its committed offset."""
218	        self._require_topic(topic_name)
219	        return self._require_group(group_name).committed_offset(topic_name)
220	
221	    # ── private guards ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
222	
223	    def _require_topic(self, topic_name: str) -> Topic:
224	        # WHY: one place that turns an unknown name into the typed error, so
225	        #      every public method validates the topic the same way.
226	        topic = self._topics.get(topic_name)
227	
228	        if topic is None:
229	            raise UnknownTopicError(f"no such topic: {topic_name}")
230	
231	        return topic
232	
233	    def _require_group(self, group_name: str) -> ConsumerGroup:
234	        group = self._groups.get(group_name)
235	
236	        if group is None:
237	            raise UnknownConsumerGroupError(f"no such consumer group: {group_name}")
238	
239	        return group
240	
241	
242	# ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
243	# The §9 "second version". The hot path (publish/poll) was ALREADY thread-safe
244	# in the core because every append/read happens while holding the topic's
245	# Condition lock. What the clean core leaves unguarded is the REGISTRY: two
246	# threads calling create_topic or subscribe at the same instant could both
247	# mutate the _topics / _groups dicts and corrupt them. This subclass overrides
248	# the one registry hook to wrap those mutations in a real lock — nothing else
249	# changes.
250	# 🎙️ "The publish/poll path was already safe — the condition variable owns a
251	#     lock. The only thing left to guard is two threads adding a topic or a
252	#     group at the same time, so the thread-safe version locks the registry."
253	class ThreadSafeMessageBroker(MessageBroker):
254	    """Adds a registry lock so concurrent create_topic / subscribe are safe."""
255	
256	    def __init__(self, clock: Clock | None = None) -> None:
257	        super().__init__(clock)
258	        # WHY: a single lock for the two registry dicts. It is only ever held
259	        #      for a couple of dict writes, never across the blocking poll, so
260	        #      it can't throttle the hot path.
261	        self._registry_lock = Lock()
262	
263	    @contextmanager
264	    def _registry_guard(self) -> Iterator[None]:
265	        # WHY: the ONLY override. Wrap registry mutations so two threads can't
266	        #      corrupt the dicts while inserting a new key at the same instant.
267	        # WHY: try/finally via the `with` statement guarantees the lock is
268	        #      released even if the body raises a DuplicateTopicError.
269	        with self._registry_lock:
270	            yield
271

2	"""
3	gateways.py — the seams to the outside world.
4	
5	The only external dependency this broker has is the wall clock: every message
6	gets a timestamp. We inject it so a test can stamp deterministic times instead
7	of depending on the real clock.
8	
9	WHY inject the clock?  The core broker never calls time.time() directly. It
10	asks the injected Clock. In tests we hand it a FakeClock we control, so a
11	message published "now" has a known, asserted timestamp — no flakiness.
12	"""
13	
14	import time
15	from typing import Protocol
16	
17	
18	# ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
19	# A tiny interface so the broker depends on the ABSTRACTION "something that can
20	# tell the time", not on the concrete time module. This is Dependency Inversion
21	# in one method.
22	# 🎙️ "The broker depends on a Clock interface, not on time.time(). That's the
23	#     seam that makes timestamps testable."
24	class Clock(Protocol):
25	    """Anything that can report the current time as a float."""
26	
27	    def now(self) -> float:
28	        """Return the current time in seconds since the epoch."""
29	        ...
30	
31	
32	# ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
33	# The real production clock — a thin wrapper over time.time().
34	class SystemClock:
35	    """The real wall clock used in production."""
36	
37	    def now(self) -> float:
38	        # WHY: the ONE place the real clock is read. Everything else takes a
39	        #      Clock, so this is the single line tests swap out.
40	        return time.time()
41

6	It walks 5 cases and prints "Case N ... -> OK" for each. A green run means the
7	broker behaves correctly on the happy path AND the tricky paths (independent

12	import time

14	from broker_service import MessageBroker, ThreadSafeMessageBroker
15	from gateways import Clock
16	from models import (

25	class FakeClock(Clock):
26	    """A clock the demo controls, so message timestamps are deterministic."""

28	    def __init__(self, start: float = 1000.0) -> None:
29	        self._t = start
30	
31	    def now(self) -> float:
32	        return self._t
33	
34	    def advance(self, seconds: float) -> None:
35	        self._t += seconds

38	def case1_two_groups_independent_offsets() -> None:
39	    print("Case 1: two groups read the same topic independently")
40	    broker = MessageBroker(clock=FakeClock())
41	    broker.create_topic("orders")
42	    broker.subscribe("billing", "orders")
43	    broker.subscribe("analytics", "orders")
44	
45	    broker.publish("orders", "order-1")
46	    broker.publish("orders", "order-2")
47	
48	    # billing reads both
49	    assert broker.poll("billing", "orders").payload == "order-1"
50	    assert broker.poll("billing", "orders").payload == "order-2"
51	    # analytics reads the SAME two from the start — independent cursor
52	    assert broker.poll("analytics", "orders").payload == "order-1"
53	    assert broker.poll("analytics", "orders").payload == "order-2"
54	
55	    assert broker.committed_offset("billing", "orders") == 2
56	    assert broker.committed_offset("analytics", "orders") == 2
57	    print("  both groups saw order-1 then order-2; offsets at 2 -> OK")
58	
59	
60	def case2_blocking_poll_unblocks_on_publish() -> None:
61	    print("Case 2: a blocking poll wakes up when a publish arrives")
62	    broker = MessageBroker(clock=FakeClock())
63	    broker.create_topic("events")
64	    broker.subscribe("worker", "events")
65	
66	    received: list[str] = []
67	
68	    def consume() -> None:
69	        # WHY: no message yet — this blocks inside cond.wait until publish wakes it.
70	        msg = broker.poll("worker", "events", timeout=5.0)
71	        received.append(msg.payload)
72	
73	    t = threading.Thread(target=consume)
74	    t.start()
75	    time.sleep(0.05)  # let the consumer reach cond.wait and block
76	    assert received == [], "consumer should still be blocked, nothing published yet"
77	
78	    broker.publish("events", "ping")
79	    t.join(timeout=2.0)
80	    assert received == ["ping"], f"expected ['ping'], got {received}"
81	    print("  consumer blocked, then woke on publish and read 'ping' -> OK")
82	
83	
84	def case3_multiple_topics_isolated() -> None:
85	    print("Case 3: multiple topics are isolated logs")
86	    broker = MessageBroker(clock=FakeClock())
87	    broker.create_topic("topic-a")
88	    broker.create_topic("topic-b")
89	    broker.subscribe("g", "topic-a")
90	    broker.subscribe("g", "topic-b")

97	    assert broker.poll("g", "topic-a").payload == "a2"

101	    print("  topic-a and topic-b kept independent logs -> OK")
102

104	def case4_error_paths() -> None:
105	    print("Case 4: every error path raises a specific typed error")
106	    broker = MessageBroker(clock=FakeClock())
107	    broker.create_topic("t")
108	    broker.subscribe("grp", "t")
109	
110	    try:
111	        broker.create_topic("t")
112	        raise AssertionError("expected DuplicateTopicError")
113	    except DuplicateTopicError:
114	        pass
115	
116	    try:
117	        broker.publish("missing", "x")
118	        raise AssertionError("expected UnknownTopicError")
119	    except UnknownTopicError:
120	        pass
121	
122	    try:
123	        broker.poll("ghost", "t", timeout=0.01)
124	        raise AssertionError("expected UnknownConsumerGroupError")
125	    except UnknownConsumerGroupError:
126	        pass
127	
128	    try:
129	        broker.publish("t", "")
130	        raise AssertionError("expected EmptyPayloadError")
131	    except EmptyPayloadError:
132	        pass
133	
134	    try:
135	        broker.poll("grp", "t", timeout=0.05)
136	        raise AssertionError("expected PollTimeoutError")
137	    except PollTimeoutError:
138	        pass
139	
140	    print("  DuplicateTopic, UnknownTopic, UnknownGroup, EmptyPayload, PollTimeout -> OK")
141	
142	
143	def case5_concurrent_producers_and_groups() -> None:
144	    print("Case 5: N producers + M groups race; every group reads all N in order")
145	    broker = ThreadSafeMessageBroker(clock=FakeClock())
146	    broker.create_topic("stream")
147	
148	    num_messages = 200
149	    group_names = ["g1", "g2", "g3"]
150	
151	    for g in group_names:
152	        broker.subscribe(g, "stream")
153	
154	    start = threading.Barrier(len(group_names) + 1)  # producers(1 driver) + M consumers
155	    seen: dict[str, list[str]] = {g: [] for g in group_names}
156	
157	    def consume(group: str) -> None:
158	        start.wait()  # all consumers + producer start together
159	        for _ in range(num_messages):
160	            msg = broker.poll(group, "stream", timeout=10.0)
161	            seen[group].append(msg.payload)
162	
163	    def produce() -> None:
164	        start.wait()
165	        for i in range(num_messages):
166	            broker.publish("stream", f"m-{i}")
167	
168	    consumers = [threading.Thread(target=consume, args=(g,)) for g in group_names]
169	    producer = threading.Thread(target=produce)
170	    for c in consumers:
171	        c.start()
172	    producer.start()
173	    for c in consumers:
174	        c.join(timeout=15.0)
175	    producer.join(timeout=15.0)
176	
177	    expected = [f"m-{i}" for i in range(num_messages)]
178	    for g in group_names:
179	        assert seen[g] == expected, f"group {g} read out of order or lost messages"
180	        assert broker.committed_offset(g, "stream") == num_messages
181	
182	    print(
183	        f"  {len(group_names)} groups each read all {num_messages} messages in order, "
184	        f"offsets all at {num_messages} -> OK"
185	    )
186	
187	
188	def main() -> None:
189	    case1_two_groups_independent_offsets()
190	    case2_blocking_poll_unblocks_on_publish()
191	    case3_multiple_topics_isolated()
192	    case4_error_paths()
193	    case5_concurrent_producers_and_groups()
194	    print("\nAll cases passed.")
195	
196	
197	if __name__ == "__main__":
198	    main()

1	# Design a Message Broker (Pub-Sub)
2	
3	> **Problem statement:** Design an in-memory, thread-safe topic-based message broker (Kafka-style pub/sub): producers publish messages to a topic; multiple consumer groups subscribe and each consumer reads from its own committed OFFSET so every group sees every message independently; consumers block until a new message arrives. Support multiple topics.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## ⏱️ 60-Minute Interview Plan
8	
9	| Time | What you do | Mode |
10	|------|-------------|------|
11	| 0–5 min | **Say §1–2 out loud** — restate "Kafka-style log, per-group offsets, blocking reads", ask clarifying questions. Don't write yet. | 🗣️ SAY |

29	## 1. Understanding the Problem & Clarifying Questions
30	
31	### 1.1 What we're building (plain words)
32	
33	> 💬 "So this is a mini Kafka. Producers throw messages at a named topic, and the topic just keeps an append-only log — message 0, message 1, message 2, forever. The clever bit is the reader side: a *consumer group* is basically a bookmark. Each group remembers how far into the log it has read, so two different groups reading the same topic see the exact same messages, totally independently — billing and analytics both get every order, neither steps on the other. And if a consumer asks for the next message but there isn't one yet, it just goes to sleep and wakes up the instant a producer publishes."
34	
35	If you've never used Kafka, that's the whole mental model: **a topic is a shared notebook everyone writes to the end of; each reader keeps their own bookmark.**
36	
37	### 1.2 Clarifying questions (candidate-led proposals)
38	
39	**You:** "I'll model this Kafka-style: each topic is one append-only log, and a consumer group is a cursor — an offset — into that log. Reading just means 'give me the message at my offset, then bump my offset'. That's the core abstraction. Sound right?"
40	**Interviewer:** "Yep, that's exactly the model."
41	*✓ locked: log + per-group offset is the spine of the whole design.*
42	
43	**You:** "For 'every group sees every message independently' — I'll make each group track its OWN offset per topic. So group A at offset 3 and group B at offset 0 read the same log with no interference. New groups start at offset 0 and see the full history. OK?"
44	**Interviewer:** "Good. Starting at 0 is fine for v1."
45	*✓ locked: independence comes from per-group cursors, not per-group copies of the data.*
46	
47	**You:** "For the blocking read — when a consumer polls and there's nothing new, I'll have it block on a condition variable until a publish appends and notifies. I'll also expose an optional timeout so a caller isn't stuck forever. Reasonable?"
48	**Interviewer:** "Yes, and the timeout is a nice touch."
49	*✓ locked: condition-variable wait/notify, plus a timeout escape hatch.*
50	
51	**You:** "I'll keep this single-process and in-memory — no disk durability, no network protocol, no partitions. If the process dies, the log is gone. That's the scope for an LLD round, agreed?"
52	**Interviewer:** "Agreed — durability and partitioning are out of scope."
53	*✓ scoped out: persistence, partitions, the wire protocol — I'll mention how they'd extend in §9.*
54	
55	**You:** "One policy call: if a producer tries to publish an empty payload, or someone polls an unknown topic or group, I'll reject with a specific typed error rather than silently no-op. I never want a publish to wake a blocked consumer on garbage. OK?"
56	**Interviewer:** "Sounds good — fail loud at the boundary."
57	*✓ locked: typed errors at every public boundary; no silent drops.*
58	
59	**You:** "Last one — within a single consumer group, can multiple threads share the work? I'd like the group's offset to be the single source of truth so even if eight threads of one group poll at once, each message is handed to the group exactly once. Worth supporting?"
60	**Interviewer:** "Yes, support that — it's the realistic case."
61	*✓ locked: the offset advance is atomic under the topic lock, so same-group threads never double-read.*
62	
63	### 1.3 Final Requirements (the whiteboard summary)
64	
65	**Functional Requirements**
66	
67	1. **Create a topic** by name.
68	   - First call creates an empty append-only log; a duplicate name is rejected with `DuplicateTopicError`.
69	   - An empty topic name is rejected with `EmptyPayloadError`.
70	2. **Subscribe a consumer group** to a topic.
71	   - A brand-new group starts at offset 0 (sees full history).
72	   - Re-subscribing an existing group must NOT rewind its offset (idempotent).
73	   - Subscribing to an unknown topic raises `UnknownTopicError`.
74	3. **Publish a message** to a topic.
75	   - Appends to the log at the next dense offset; returns the stored `Message`.
76	   - Wakes every consumer blocked on that topic.
77	   - Empty payload → `EmptyPayloadError`; unknown topic → `UnknownTopicError`.
78	4. **Poll the next message** for a group on a topic, BLOCKING until one exists.
79	   - Returns the message at the group's committed offset, then advances the offset by one (commit).
80	   - If a `timeout` is given and elapses with nothing new → `PollTimeoutError`.
81	   - Unknown topic/group → `UnknownTopicError` / `UnknownConsumerGroupError`.
82	5. **Support multiple topics** — each topic is an isolated log with its own offsets and its own condition variable.
83	6. **Thread-safe** — concurrent producers and consumer-group threads never lose, duplicate, or reorder a message for any group.
84	
85	**Out of scope**
86	- Disk durability / replication (in-memory only — process death loses the log).
87	- Partitions and the network wire protocol (single-process library).
88	- Consumer-group rebalancing / partition assignment (single logical partition per topic).
89	- Message TTL / log compaction / retention (the log grows unbounded in v1).
90	
91	> 💬 **What you say to open:** "OK, before I model anything I want to lock down the spine: a topic is one append-only log, a consumer group is a cursor into it, and a blocking poll is a condition-variable wait. With that, let me walk through the actors and flows."

95	## 2. Actors & Core Flows
96	
97	**Actors**
98	- **Producer** — calls `publish(topic, payload)` to append a message. Initiates the publish flow.
99	- **Consumer (within a Consumer Group)** — calls `poll(group, topic)` to read the next message; blocks if none. Initiates the read flow.
100	- **Admin / setup code** — calls `create_topic` and `subscribe` to wire the system up.
101	- **Clock (external seam)** — supplies the timestamp stamped on each message; injected so tests are deterministic.
102	
103	**Core flows**
104	1. Setup: admin `create_topic("orders")` → group `subscribe("billing", "orders")` → cursor starts at offset 0.
105	2. Publish: producer `publish("orders", "o1")` → append at offset 0 → `notify_all` wakes blocked consumers.
106	3. Poll (data ready): consumer `poll("billing", "orders")` → read `log[0]` → commit offset → 1 → return message.
107	4. Poll (blocking): consumer `poll("analytics", "orders")` with empty log → `cond.wait()` → sleeps → producer publishes → consumer wakes, reads, commits.
108	5. Independence: group `billing` at offset 5 and group `analytics` at offset 0 read the same log with no interference.
109

112	## 3. Core Entities (with explicit accept / reject reasoning)
113	
114	### 3.1 Candidate noun walkthrough (accept / reject)
115	
116	> **Message** — A published record: payload, the topic it belongs to, its permanent offset, and a timestamp. It is referenced by the log and returned to consumers, and it never changes after publish. *Accepted — a frozen value object.*
117	>
118	> **Topic** — A real thing with state (the append-only log) AND behavior (append, report next offset). It also owns the condition variable that producers/consumers coordinate on. *Accepted.*
119	>
120	> **ConsumerGroup** — Tempting to call this "just an int offset" and stash it in a map on the broker. But a group has its OWN state — an offset PER topic — and that state is the entire reason groups read independently. Giving it its own object keeps each cursor self-contained. *Accepted.*
121	>
122	> **Log** — The append-only sequence of messages. It has no behavior beyond "append" and "index by offset", and it's intrinsic to exactly one topic. *Rejected as its own entity — it's a `list[Message]` field on `Topic`.*
123	>
124	> **Offset** — A consumer group's read position. A finite integer with no behavior of its own. *Rejected — it's an `int` field inside `ConsumerGroup.offsets`.*
125	>
126	> **Producer / Consumer** — Actors. They CALL `publish` / `poll`; the broker doesn't store them. A consumer is just a thread that happens to be polling on behalf of a group. *Rejected — actors live outside the model.*
127	>
128	> **Partition** — In real Kafka a topic is split into partitions for parallelism. We scoped that out — one logical partition per topic. *Rejected — out of scope for v1, noted as a §9 extension.*
129	>
130	> **Clock** — Supplies timestamps. It's an external dependency, not domain state. *Rejected as an entity — modeled as an injected `Clock` gateway.*
131	
132	### 3.2 Final entity table (accepted entities only)
133	
134	| Entity | Responsibility | Key fields (typed) | Invariant it guards |
135	|---|---|---|---|
136	| `MessageBroker` | Coordinate topics + groups; route publish/poll | `_topics: dict[str, Topic]`, `_groups: dict[str, ConsumerGroup]`, `_clock: Clock` | A topic name maps to exactly one log; an unknown name is a typed error, never an auto-create |
137	| `Topic` | Hold one append-only log + its condition variable | `name: str`, `log: list[Message]`, `cond: Condition` | Offsets are dense and 0-based: `next_offset() == len(log)`, always |
138	| `ConsumerGroup` | Track this group's committed offset per topic | `name: str`, `offsets: dict[str, int]` | Offset only moves forward (commit); re-subscribe never rewinds it |
139	| `Message` | An immutable published record | `offset: int`, `topic: str`, `payload: str`, `timestamp: float` | Frozen — once at offset *k*, always at offset *k* |
140	
141	**Enums:** none needed. The states here are integer offsets, not a finite labeled set — modeling them as an enum would be wrong.
142	
143	### 3.3 🎙️ Spoken intro
144	
145	> 🎙️ "So the model is four objects. `MessageBroker` is the brain — it owns the topics and the groups and routes each call to the right place. `Topic` is the interesting one: it's an append-only log plus a condition variable, and that condition variable is what a blocking poll sleeps on and a publish wakes. `ConsumerGroup` is just a named cursor — it remembers its offset per topic, and that's the whole reason every group reads independently. `Message` is a frozen record that lives in the log forever. The nouns I deliberately left out are Log and Offset — those are a list and an int inside Topic and ConsumerGroup — and Partition, which I scoped out as a §9 extension."

149	## 3.5 API / System Interface (the public surface, before the classes)
150	
151	The orchestrator's public surface is **four methods** — two write paths, one read path, one setup call. Plus two read-only inspectors for tests/observability.
152	
153	> | Method | Signature | Returns | Raises | Purpose |
154	> |---|---|---|---|---|
155	> | `create_topic` | `(name: str) -> None` | — | `EmptyPayloadError`, `DuplicateTopicError` | Admin creates a new empty append-only log |
156	> | `subscribe` | `(group_name: str, topic_name: str) -> ConsumerGroup` | the group cursor | `EmptyPayloadError`, `UnknownTopicError` | A consumer group starts following a topic (offset 0) |
157	> | `publish` | `(topic_name: str, payload: str) -> Message` | the stored message | `EmptyPayloadError`, `UnknownTopicError` | A producer appends a message and wakes blocked consumers |
158	> | `poll` | `(group_name: str, topic_name: str, timeout: float \| None = None) -> Message` | next message for the group | `UnknownTopicError`, `UnknownConsumerGroupError`, `PollTimeoutError` | A consumer reads the next message, BLOCKING until one arrives |
159	> | `topic_size` | `(topic_name: str) -> int` | count of published messages | `UnknownTopicError` | Inspect how many messages a topic holds (== next offset) |
160	> | `committed_offset` | `(group_name: str, topic_name: str) -> int` | the group's offset | `UnknownTopicError`, `UnknownConsumerGroupError` | Inspect how far a group has read |
161	
162	> 🎙️ "The whole system is four real calls: `create_topic` and `subscribe` to set up, `publish` to write, and `poll` to read — and `poll` is the only one that can block. The two `*_offset`/`*_size` getters are just windows for observability and tests. If you handed me this interface cold, you'd know immediately: it's an append-only log with per-group cursors and a blocking reader."

167	
168	### 4.1 Per-class derivation
169	
170	#### `MessageBroker` (the orchestrator)
171	
172	**(a) One-line role** — The public API: external code calls it to create topics, subscribe groups, publish, and poll. Everything routes through here.
173	
174	**(b) State derivation**
175	
176	| Requirement | What this class must remember |
177	|---|---|
178	| "Support multiple topics" | A map: topic name → `Topic` |
179	| "Multiple consumer groups subscribe" | A map: group name → `ConsumerGroup` |
180	| "Each message gets a timestamp" (deterministic in tests) | An injected `Clock` |
181	
182	```
183	class MessageBroker:
184	    - _topics: dict[str, Topic]
185	    - _groups: dict[str, ConsumerGroup]
186	    - _clock: Clock
187	```
188	
189	*State placement trade-off:* the per-group OFFSET is relational state — it describes "where THIS group has read in THIS topic", a system-managed relationship — so it lives on `ConsumerGroup`, not on `Topic`. The LOG is physical to a topic, so it lives on `Topic`. I keep offsets off the broker so each group advances independently without a central bottleneck.
190	
191	**(c) Operations derivation**
192	
193	| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
194	|---|---|
195	| "Support multiple topics" | `create_topic(name) -> None` |
196	| "Consumer groups subscribe" | `subscribe(group_name, topic_name) -> ConsumerGroup` |
197	| "Producers publish messages" | `publish(topic_name, payload) -> Message` |
198	| "Consumers read from their offset, blocking" | `poll(group_name, topic_name, timeout) -> Message` |
199	| Observe log length / group progress | `topic_size`, `committed_offset` |
200	
201	```
202	class MessageBroker:
203	    - _topics: dict[str, Topic]
204	    - _groups: dict[str, ConsumerGroup]
205	    - _clock: Clock
206	
207	    + MessageBroker(clock: Clock | None = None)
208	    + create_topic(name: str) -> None
209	    + subscribe(group_name: str, topic_name: str) -> ConsumerGroup
210	    + publish(topic_name: str, payload: str) -> Message
211	    + poll(group_name: str, topic_name: str, timeout: float | None = None) -> Message
212	    + topic_size(topic_name: str) -> int
213	    + committed_offset(group_name: str, topic_name: str) -> int

215	    # _registry_guard() -> ContextManager       (concurrency seam #2: no-op in core)
216	    # _require_topic(name) -> Topic
217	    # _require_group(name) -> ConsumerGroup
218	```
219	
220	**(d) Design choices worth calling out**
221	- **Why does `subscribe` return the `ConsumerGroup`?** So the caller can inspect its cursor directly if needed; the broker still keeps the canonical reference in `_groups`.
222	- **Why no auto-create on publish to an unknown topic?** A typo would silently create a ghost topic and blocked consumers would never wake. Explicit `create_topic` + a typed error is safer.
223	- **Why two concurrency seams (`_topic_cv` and `_registry_guard`)?** The hot path (publish/poll) is already serialized by the topic's condition lock — that's not optional, blocking IS the feature. The only thing left unguarded in the clean core is the registry dicts, so `_registry_guard` is the no-op hook the thread-safe subclass overrides.
224	
225	#### `Topic` (the log + condition variable)
226	
227	**(a) One-line role** — Holds one topic's append-only log and the condition variable that producers notify and consumers wait on.
228	
229	**(b) State derivation**
230	
231	| Requirement | What this class must remember |
232	|---|---|
233	| "Append-only log per topic" | `log: list[Message]` |
234	| "Consumers block until a new message arrives" | `cond: Condition` (wait/notify) |
235	| Routing by name | `name: str` |
236	
237	```
238	class Topic:
239	    - name: str
240	    - log: list[Message]
241	    - cond: Condition
242	```
243	
244	**(c) Operations derivation**
245	
246	| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
247	|---|---|
248	| "Each message gets the next dense offset" | `next_offset() -> int` |
249	
250	```
251	class Topic:
252	    - name: str
253	    - log: list[Message]
254	    - cond: Condition
255	
256	    + Topic(name: str)
257	    + next_offset() -> int      # == len(log); the offset the next message gets
258	```

260	**(d) Design choices worth calling out**
261	- **Why is the `Condition` ON the topic?** Both `publish` (notify) and `poll` (wait) need the SAME object, scoped to one topic, so two topics never block each other. Putting it on the entity makes that scoping automatic.
262	- **Why no explicit offset counter?** `len(log)` IS the next offset — offsets are dense and 0-based — so there is no second counter to drift out of sync.
263

265	
266	**(a) One-line role** — A named subscriber that remembers its own committed read offset per topic; this is what makes every group read independently.

270	| Requirement | What this class must remember |
271	|---|---|
272	| "Each group reads from its own committed offset" | `offsets: dict[str, int]` (per topic) |

296	**(d) Design choices worth calling out**
297	- **Why a dict of offsets, not a single int?** One group can follow many topics; each needs its own independent cursor.

390	| `ConsumerGroup` (per-group offset) | Observer-style independent cursors | Each group observes the same log at its own pace, decoupled from producers and from each other |
391	| `Clock` / `SystemClock` | Injected dependency (Dependency Inversion) | Broker depends on a `Clock` abstraction, not on `time.time()`, so timestamps are testable |

Below, `ThreadSafeMessageBroker` extends the broker and overrides one method — that's the concurrency seam. The blocking read is NOT a bug to fix later — it's the feature, and it's a condition-variable wait/notify: `poll` sleeps on the topic's `Condition`, `publish` appends and `notify_all`s. Holding that condition's lock already makes the append and the read-then-commit atomic, so the hot path is correct by construction. The ONE thing the clean core leaves unguarded is the topic/group *registry* — two threads calling `create_topic` or `subscribe` at the same instant could corrupt the dicts. I'll keep the core clean and harden exactly that registry in §9 with a small lock — not a coarse lock over the whole broker."

400	## 5. Design Patterns & Principles
401	
402	### 5.1 Information Expert
403	
404	> "I followed Information Expert: each class owns the state and behavior closest to its data. `Topic` owns its log AND the condition variable, because the topic is the only thing that knows when a new message arrived and who should wake. `ConsumerGroup` owns its offset, because 'how far have I read' is intrinsic to the group, not to the log or the broker. `MessageBroker` owns only the relationships — which topics and groups exist — because it's the one object that sees all of them. Offsets deliberately do NOT live on the broker; if they did, every group would funnel through one object and lose its independence."
405	
406	### 5.2 Applied patterns
407	
408	**Condition-variable (monitor) wait/notify**
409	- **Why it fits HERE:** "consumers block until a new message arrives" is *exactly* the producer/consumer signalling problem a condition variable solves. A consumer at the head of the log must sleep without busy-waiting and wake the instant a producer appends. The topic's `Condition` is the shared monitor: `poll` does `cond.wait_for(predicate)`, `publish` does `cond.notify_all()`.
410	- **Interface + impl:** `Topic.cond: threading.Condition`; `poll` waits, `publish` notifies — same object, per topic.
411	- 🎙️ **Script:** "The blocking poll is a textbook condition variable. The consumer holds the topic's lock and waits on the condition while there's nothing at its offset — `wait_for` releases the lock and sleeps. A publish takes the same lock, appends, and `notify_all`s, which wakes every blocked group to re-check its own offset."
412	- 💬 **Layman gloss:** "It's like a reader napping next to a notebook with a bell on it. Instead of checking the notebook every second, they sleep; whoever writes a new line rings the bell and the reader wakes up to read it."
413	
414	**Injected Clock (Dependency Inversion)**
415	- **Why it fits HERE:** every message gets a timestamp, and a test that asserts on timestamps can't depend on the real wall clock. Injecting a `Clock` makes `publish` deterministic.
416	- **Interface + impl:** `Clock.now() -> float`; `SystemClock` in prod, `FakeClock` in tests.
417	- 🎙️ **Script:** "The broker never calls `time.time()` directly — it asks the injected clock. In a test I hand it a fake clock and assert the exact timestamp."
418	- 💬 **Layman gloss:** "The broker doesn't own a watch — someone hands it one. In tests we hand it a stopped watch so the time is whatever we say."
419	
420	### 5.3 SOLID principles
421	- **Single Responsibility:** `Topic` owns the log + signalling, `ConsumerGroup` owns the cursor, `MessageBroker` owns coordination. Three reasons to change, three classes.
422	- **Open/Closed:** the thread-safe variant is a *new subclass* (`ThreadSafeMessageBroker`) overriding one hook — the clean core never changes.
423	- **Dependency Inversion:** `MessageBroker` depends on the `Clock` abstraction, never on `time.time()` directly.
424	
425	### 5.4 EARN YOUR PATTERNS
426	- The condition variable is real: `Topic.cond` with `wait_for`/`notify_all` in `broker_service.py` — `Cmd-F` finds it.
427	- The injected clock is real: `Clock` Protocol in `gateways.py`, swapped for `FakeClock` in tests.
428	- The Template Method hook is real: `_registry_guard` is a no-op `@contextmanager` in the core, overridden in `ThreadSafeMessageBroker`.
429	- I do **NOT** claim a GoF "Observer" object — there's no `Observer` interface with `update()`. The per-group cursor gives observer-LIKE independent consumption, so I call it "observer-style", not a wired Observer pattern. Honesty over name-dropping.
430	
431	## 5.5 Core Algorithms & Approach
432	
433	### A. The Key Insight
434	
435	> **Insight:** A topic is an append-only LOG and each consumer group is just a CURSOR (an integer offset) into it; a blocking read is `wait` on a condition variable that `publish` `notify`s.
436	>
437	> **Why it works:** Because the log never deletes and each group keeps its own offset, "deliver every message to every group exactly once, in order" reduces to "return `log[my_offset]`, then `my_offset += 1`". There is no fan-out queue per consumer to keep in sync, no per-group copy of the data — just one shared list and N independent integers. Blocking is free: `Condition.wait_for(predicate)` atomically releases the lock and sleeps until `notify`, then re-checks the predicate, which handles spurious wakeups.
438	>
439	> **What breaks without it:** A naive version gives each consumer group its OWN queue and has `publish` push the message into every group's queue (fan-out on write). Now publishing is O(number of groups), and the instant a new group subscribes mid-stream you must decide whether to back-fill its queue with history — and if any push is missed, that group silently loses a message forever. The cursor model makes back-fill trivial (start at offset 0) and publish O(1) regardless of group count.
440	
441	### B. Per-Operation Algorithm
442	
443	**`publish(topic_name, payload)`**
444	
445	(1) Pseudocode:
446	```
447	1. if payload is empty -> raise EmptyPayloadError
448	2. topic = require_topic(topic_name)          # unknown -> UnknownTopicError
449	3. cv = topic's condition variable
450	4. acquire cv (lock):
451	5.     offset = len(topic.log)                # the next dense offset
452	6.     msg = Message(offset, topic, payload, clock.now())
453	7.     topic.log.append(msg)                  # the critical append
454	8.     cv.notify_all()                        # wake every blocked group
455	9. release cv
456	10. return msg
457	```
458	
459	(2) 🎙️ **Walkthrough — the write path:** "This is the producer side. Steps 1–2 are boundary guards. The critical section is 5–8 under the topic's lock: compute the offset as `len(log)`, append, and `notify_all`. Step 8 is the one that matters — `notify_all`, not `notify`, because SEVERAL consumer groups may be blocked on this topic and each must wake to check whether the new message is at ITS offset. The append and offset-compute are one atomic step, so two producers can't grab the same offset."
460	
461	(3) **Dry run — `publish("orders", "o3")`** with `log=[o0,o1,o2]`, group `billing` blocked in `poll` at offset 3:
462	
463	| Step | Action | State change |
464	|---|---|---|
465	| 1 | payload "o3" non-empty | passes guard |
466	| 2 | require_topic("orders") | found ✓ |
467	| 5 | offset = len(log) | offset = 3 |
468	| 6 | build Message | `Message(3, "orders", "o3", 1042.0)` |
469	| 7 | log.append | `log=[o0,o1,o2,o3]`, next_offset → 4 |
470	| 8 | cv.notify_all | `billing` wakes, re-checks `4 > 3` → True |
471	| 10 | return msg | producer gets `Message(offset=3)` |
472	
473	> *Edge probe: two producers publish at once?* Both contend for the topic lock; one appends at offset 3 and releases, the other then appends at offset 4. No collision — `len(log)` is read inside the lock.
474	
475	**`poll(group_name, topic_name, timeout)`** — the blocking read
476	
477	(1) Pseudocode:
478	```
479	1. topic = require_topic(topic_name)          # unknown -> UnknownTopicError
480	2. group = require_group(group_name)          # unknown -> UnknownConsumerGroupError
481	3. cv = topic's condition variable
482	4. acquire cv (lock):
483	5.     ready = cv.wait_for(lambda: len(topic.log) > group.offset[topic], timeout)
484	6.     if not ready -> raise PollTimeoutError   # timed out, nothing new
485	7.     off = group.offset[topic]
486	8.     msg = topic.log[off]                    # read at my cursor
487	9.     group.offset[topic] = off + 1           # commit: advance my cursor
488	10. release cv
489	11. return msg
490	```
491	
492	(2) 🎙️ **Walkthrough — the race-prone read, made safe:** "This is the read path and it's where blocking lives. Step 5 is the heart: `wait_for` checks the predicate 'is the log longer than my offset?'. If yes, it returns immediately; if no, it releases the lock and sleeps until a publish notifies, then re-checks — that re-check is why spurious wakeups don't hurt us. Steps 7–9 are the commit: read at my offset, then bump my offset by one. Holding the lock across read-then-commit is what guarantees exactly-once even if eight threads of the SAME group poll at once — only one gets offset `off`, the next gets `off+1`."
493	
494	**`create_topic` / `subscribe` / `topic_size` / `committed_offset`** (the rest):
495	```
496	create_topic(name):  guard empty -> [registry_guard] if exists raise Duplicate else topics[name]=Topic(name)
497	subscribe(g,t):      guard empty; require_topic(t); [registry_guard] groups.setdefault(g); group.offsets.setdefault(t,0); return group
498	topic_size(t):       require_topic(t); return len(log)
499	committed_offset(g,t): require_topic(t); return require_group(g).offsets.get(t,0)
500	```
501	🎙️ For `subscribe`: "The one subtlety is `setdefault`, not assignment — re-subscribing a group that already read message 5 must NOT reset it to 0, or it re-reads everything."
502	
503	### C. Data Structure Choices
504	
505	| Data Structure | Used For | Why THIS (vs alternative considered) | Complexity |
506	|---|---|---|---|
507	| `list[Message]` per topic | the append-only log | index == offset, so a consumer at offset k reads `log[k]` directly; a fan-out queue per group would be O(groups) on every publish and risk losing back-fill for late subscribers | append O(1) amortised, read O(1) |
508	| `dict[str, int]` per group | committed offsets per topic | one group follows many topics, each needs its own cursor; a single int can't model multi-topic membership | O(1) get/set |
509	| `dict[str, Topic]` / `dict[str, ConsumerGroup]` | the registries | O(1) routing of publish/poll/subscribe by name; a list would be O(n) per call | O(1) |
510	| `threading.Condition` per topic | wait/notify | the idiomatic Python primitive for "sleep until a predicate holds"; busy-wait + `sleep(0.01)` burns CPU and adds latency jitter | wait/notify O(waiters) on notify |
511	
512	I deliberately chose a **plain list as the log** over a ring buffer / segmented log. I'd switch to a bounded ring buffer (with retention) once a topic crosses memory limits — the list grows unbounded in v1, which is fine for an in-memory interview scope.
513	
514	### D. Tricky Logic
515	
516	> **🧠 Claim:** `poll` must use `wait_for(predicate)`, not a bare `wait()`.
517	> **🔬 Proof:** A bare `wait()` returns on ANY `notify` and on spurious wakeups. If group A's message arrives and `notify_all` wakes group B too, group B's bare `wait` would fall through and try to read `log[its_offset]` — which doesn't exist yet — an IndexError or a wrong read.
518	> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `cv.wait_for(lambda: topic.next_offset() > group.committed_offset(...))` re-checks the per-group predicate on every wakeup and only returns when THIS group actually has a message.
519	
520	> **🧠 Claim:** read-then-commit must be ONE atomic step under the topic lock.
521	> **🔬 Proof:** If two threads of one group both read `log[off]` before either commits, both return the SAME message and the group then jumps to `off+1`, silently skipping the message that should have been at `off+1` for one of them — a duplicate AND a loss.
522	> **🛠️ Fix in code:** steps 7–9 of `poll` run entirely inside `with cv:`. One thread reads `off` and sets `off+1` before the next thread re-evaluates.
523	
524	> **🧠 Claim:** `notify_all`, never `notify`.
525	> **🔬 Proof:** `notify` wakes ONE arbitrary waiter. If groups A and B both block on the topic and a publish wakes only A, B sleeps forever even though the message is at B's offset too.
526	> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `publish` calls `cv.notify_all()` so every group re-checks its own offset.
527	
528	> **🧠 Claim:** re-subscribe must be idempotent (no rewind).
529	> **🔬 Proof:** Setup code or a reconnect might call `subscribe(g, t)` again. Plain `offsets[t] = 0` would rewind a group that already read 100 messages, replaying all of them.
530	> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `group.offsets.setdefault(topic.name, 0)` — only sets 0 if the key is absent.
531	
532	> **🧠 Claim:** the registry needs its own lock — but only that.
533	> **🔬 Proof:** Two threads `create_topic("x")` at once: both pass `if name not in topics`, both insert — and one Topic (with any blocked consumers) is lost. The hot path doesn't have this problem because the condition's lock already serializes appends.
534	> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `ThreadSafeMessageBroker._registry_guard` wraps the existence-check-and-insert in a `Lock`; the existence re-check happens INSIDE the guard.
535	
536	### E. The "what I'd write WRONG first" anti-example
537	
538	> **❌ Naive version a junior would write:**
539	> ```
540	> def poll(self, group, topic):
541	>     while topic.next_offset() <= group.offset[topic.name]:   # busy spin
542	>         time.sleep(0.01)                                      # poll the clock
543	>     msg = topic.log[group.offset[topic.name]]
544	>     group.offset[topic.name] += 1                             # not under any lock
545	>     return msg
546	> ```
547	> **Why it's wrong:** Two failure modes. (1) The busy spin burns a CPU core and adds up to 10ms of latency per message for nothing — a condition variable sleeps and wakes instantly on publish. (2) The read-then-increment is outside any lock, so two same-group threads can read the same offset and one message is duplicated while the next is skipped. The correct version uses `cond.wait_for` (no spin, instant wake) and does read-then-commit inside the topic lock (atomic, exactly-once).

655	# Python 3.11+
656	"""
657	models.py — the "nouns" of the message broker.
658	"""
659	
660	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
661	from threading import Condition
662	
663	
664	class BrokerError(Exception):
665	    """Base class for every error the broker raises."""
666	
667	
668	class UnknownTopicError(BrokerError):
669	    """Raised when a caller names a topic that was never created."""
670	
671	
672	class DuplicateTopicError(BrokerError):
673	    """Raised when create_topic is called twice with the same name."""
674	
675	
676	class UnknownConsumerGroupError(BrokerError):
677	    """Raised when a caller names a consumer group that never subscribed."""
678	
679	
680	class EmptyPayloadError(BrokerError):
681	    """Raised when a producer tries to publish a None/empty payload."""
682	
683	
684	class PollTimeoutError(BrokerError):
685	    """Raised when poll() waited the full timeout and no message arrived."""
686	
687	
688	@dataclass(frozen=True)
689	class Message:
690	    """One published message. Frozen — nothing mutates it after publish."""
691	
692	    offset: int          # permanent position in the topic log (0-based)
693	    topic: str           # which topic this belongs to
694	    payload: str         # the actual content the producer sent
695	    timestamp: float     # when it was appended (from the injected Clock)
696	
697	
698	@dataclass
699	class Topic:
700	    """An append-only log for one topic, plus the condition consumers wait on."""
701	
702	    name: str
703	    # WHY: a plain list IS the log. Index == offset, append is O(1) amortised.
704	    log: list[Message] = field(default_factory=list)
705	    # WHY: the Condition owns its OWN lock. Everything touching `log` happens
706	    #      while holding it, and poll() releases it while it sleeps in wait().
707	    cond: Condition = field(default_factory=Condition)
708	
709	    def next_offset(self) -> int:
710	        """The offset the NEXT appended message will get == current length."""
711	        return len(self.log)
712	
713	
714	@dataclass
715	class ConsumerGroup:
716	    """A named subscriber that tracks its own committed read offset per topic."""
717	
718	    name: str
719	    # WHY: offset PER topic — one group can follow many topics independently.
720	    offsets: dict[str, int] = field(default_factory=dict)
721	
722	    def committed_offset(self, topic_name: str) -> int:
723	        """How far this group has read in the given topic (0 if brand new)."""
724	        return self.offsets.get(topic_name, 0)

781	
782	#### The two concurrency seams
783	
784	1. **Role:** `_topic_cv` funnels both `poll` (wait) and `publish` (notify) through one named place that decides what to block on for a topic. In the clean core, the topic's own `Condition`.
785	2. **Edge cases:** none — it's a pure accessor.
786	3. **Code:**
787	```python copy
788	    def _topic_cv(self, topic: Topic):
789	        return topic.cond
790	
791	    @contextmanager
792	    def _registry_guard(self) -> Iterator[None]:
793	        yield  # does nothing — the thread-safe subclass overrides this
794	```
795	4. **Reasoning:** The blocking logic is ALREADY real here — `poll`/`publish` use `topic.cond`, which carries its own lock, so the hot path is correct even in the "clean" core. The only no-op is `_registry_guard`, the Template Method hook the thread-safe subclass replaces.
796	5. 🎙️ "My clean core has zero registry-lock code. The thread-safe version overrides exactly one method, `_registry_guard`."
797	6. ⚠️ "Why is the condition lock not also a no-op?" → "Because blocking IS the feature — I can't fake it. The condition's lock is load-bearing in the core."
798	
799	#### `publish` — the write path
800	
801	1. **Role:** appends a message and wakes every consumer blocked on the topic.
802	2. **Core logic:**
803	   1. Reject empty payload.
804	   2. Resolve the topic (typed error if unknown).
805	   3. Under the topic lock: compute next offset, build the `Message`, append.
806	   4. `notify_all` to wake every blocked group.
807	   5. Return the stored message.
808	3. **Edge cases:** empty payload (`EmptyPayloadError`), unknown topic (`UnknownTopicError`), two concurrent publishers (offset collision — prevented by the lock).
809	4. **Code:**
810	```python copy
811	    def publish(self, topic_name: str, payload: str) -> Message:
812	        """Append a message to a topic's log and wake all blocked consumers."""
813	        if not payload:
814	            raise EmptyPayloadError("cannot publish an empty payload")
815	
816	        topic = self._require_topic(topic_name)
817	        cv = self._topic_cv(topic)
818	
819	        with cv:
820	            message = Message(
821	                offset=topic.next_offset(),
822	                topic=topic.name,
823	                payload=payload,
824	                timestamp=self._clock.now(),
825	            )
826	            topic.log.append(message)
827	            # WHY: notify_all — several consumer GROUPS may be blocked, and each
828	            #      must wake to check whether this message is at ITS offset.
829	            cv.notify_all()
830	
831	        return message
832	```
833	5. **Reasoning:** The whole point is the critical section under `with cv`. `topic.next_offset()` is `len(log)`, read inside the lock, so two publishers can't claim the same offset. `notify_all` (not `notify`) is the non-obvious choice — `notify` would wake only one group and the others would sleep through a message that's at their offset too. Notice the offset is computed and the append happens atomically; there's no separate counter to drift.
834	6. 🎙️ "Append under the topic lock, then `notify_all`. The lock makes the offset-and-append one step." ⚠️ "Why notify_all not notify?" → "Multiple groups block on one topic — one wakeup per group, all re-check their own offset."
835	
836	#### `poll` — the blocking read
837	
838	1. **Role:** returns the next message for a group, sleeping until one exists.
839	2. **Core logic:**
840	   1. Resolve topic + group (typed errors if unknown).
841	   2. Under the topic lock, `wait_for` "log longer than my offset", honoring timeout.
842	   3. On timeout → `PollTimeoutError`.
843	   4. Read `log[my_offset]`, then commit `my_offset += 1`.
844	   5. Return the message.
845	3. **Edge cases:** unknown topic/group, timeout, spurious wakeup (handled by `wait_for` predicate), two same-group threads (atomic commit prevents double-read).
846	4. **Code:**
847	```python copy
848	    def poll(self, group_name: str, topic_name: str, timeout: float | None = None) -> Message:
849	        """Read the next message for a group, BLOCKING until one is available."""
850	        topic = self._require_topic(topic_name)
851	        group = self._require_group(group_name)
852	        cv = self._topic_cv(topic)
853	
854	        with cv:
855	            # WHY: wait_for re-checks the predicate on every wakeup (handles
856	            #      spurious wakeups + other groups' notifies). Predicate: there
857	            #      is a message at MY committed offset.
858	            ready = cv.wait_for(
859	                lambda: topic.next_offset() > group.committed_offset(topic.name),
860	                timeout=timeout,
861	            )
862	
863	            if not ready:
864	                raise PollTimeoutError(
865	                    f"no message for group {group_name!r} on topic {topic_name!r} "
866	                    f"within {timeout}s"
867	                )
868	
869	            # WHY: read at my cursor, then bump it — the "commit". Holding the
870	            #      lock makes read-then-commit atomic, so a message is delivered
871	            #      to this group exactly once even under many same-group threads.
872	            offset = group.committed_offset(topic.name)
873	            message = topic.log[offset]
874	            group.offsets[topic.name] = offset + 1
875	
876	        return message
877	```
878	5. **Reasoning:** `wait_for` is the senior choice — it releases the lock and sleeps, then re-evaluates the predicate on every wakeup, so a `notify_all` meant for another group doesn't make this one read a message that isn't there. The read-then-commit (steps 7–9 of the pseudocode) is inside the lock: that's what makes exactly-once hold when eight threads of one group poll together — one reads offset `off` and sets `off+1` before the next thread evaluates. Notice we do NOT check the topic exists inside the lock — that read happens before, on fields that don't change.
879	6. 🎙️ "Wait until there's a message at my offset, then read-and-commit atomically." ⚠️ "What if two threads of one group poll at once?" → "The read and the offset bump are inside the lock, so one gets the message and advances; the other reads the next one. Never the same message twice."
880	
881	### `create_topic`, `subscribe`, and helpers
882	
883	🎙️ "These wrap a registry mutation in the `_registry_guard` hook — a no-op in the core, a real lock in the thread-safe version."
884	
885	```python copy
886	    def create_topic(self, name: str) -> None:
887	        """Create a new, empty topic. Raises if the name is already taken."""
888	        if not name:
889	            raise EmptyPayloadError("topic name must be non-empty")
890	
891	        with self._registry_guard():
892	            if name in self._topics:   # re-check INSIDE the guard
893	                raise DuplicateTopicError(f"topic already exists: {name}")
894	            self._topics[name] = Topic(name=name)
895	
896	    def subscribe(self, group_name: str, topic_name: str) -> ConsumerGroup:
897	        """Subscribe a consumer group to a topic; returns the group cursor."""
898	        if not group_name:
899	            raise EmptyPayloadError("group name must be non-empty")
900	
901	        topic = self._require_topic(topic_name)
902	
903	        with self._registry_guard():
904	            group = self._groups.get(group_name)
905	
906	            if group is None:
907	                group = ConsumerGroup(name=group_name)
908	                self._groups[group_name] = group
909	
910	            # WHY: setdefault, NOT assignment — re-subscribing must not rewind
911	            #      a group that already read messages.
912	            group.offsets.setdefault(topic.name, 0)
913	
914	        return group
915	
916	    def topic_size(self, topic_name: str) -> int:
917	        """How many messages have been published to a topic (== next offset)."""
918	        return self._require_topic(topic_name).next_offset()
919	
920	    def committed_offset(self, group_name: str, topic_name: str) -> int:
921	        """How far a group has read in a topic — its committed offset."""
922	        self._require_topic(topic_name)
923	        return self._require_group(group_name).committed_offset(topic_name)
924	
925	    def _require_topic(self, topic_name: str) -> Topic:
926	        topic = self._topics.get(topic_name)
927	
928	        if topic is None:
929	            raise UnknownTopicError(f"no such topic: {topic_name}")
930	
931	        return topic
932	
933	    def _require_group(self, group_name: str) -> ConsumerGroup:
934	        group = self._groups.get(group_name)
935	
936	        if group is None:
937	            raise UnknownConsumerGroupError(f"no such consumer group: {group_name}")
938	
939	        return group

941	
942	#### Approach comparison — the blocking-read mechanism (the one interesting choice)
943	
944	**Approach 1 — busy-wait poll (rejected):**
945	```
946	while topic.next_offset() <= group.offset:
947	    time.sleep(0.01)
948	```
949	> **Challenges:** Burns a CPU core per blocked consumer and adds up to 10ms latency per message for nothing. With 100 blocked consumers that's 100 threads spinning. Latency is also jittery — a message published right after a sleep starts waits the whole interval.
950	
951	**Approach 2 — one queue per consumer group, fan-out on publish (rejected for this design):**
952	```
953	publish: for group in groups[topic]: group.queue.put(msg)
954	```
955	> **Challenges:** Publish becomes O(number of groups), and state lives in N queues that must stay in sync with the log. A group that subscribes mid-stream needs explicit back-fill, and a single missed `put` loses a message for that group forever. "I'd consider this only if groups needed per-group filtering/transforms at publish time."
956	
957	**Chosen approach — shared log + per-group cursor + condition variable:**
958	```
959	publish: append to the one log; cond.notify_all()
960	poll: cond.wait_for(log_len > my_offset); read log[my_offset]; my_offset += 1
961	```
962	> **Why it wins:** Single source of truth — one log, N integers. Publish is O(1) regardless of group count, a late subscriber just starts at offset 0 (free back-fill), and the condition variable sleeps with zero CPU and wakes instantly on publish.
963	
964	### `ThreadSafeMessageBroker` — the §9 second version
965	
966	🎙️ "One overridden hook. The hot path was already safe; this adds the registry lock."
967	
968	```python copy
969	class ThreadSafeMessageBroker(MessageBroker):
970	    """Adds a registry lock so concurrent create_topic / subscribe are safe."""
971	
972	    def __init__(self, clock: Clock | None = None) -> None:
973	        super().__init__(clock)
974	        self._registry_lock = Lock()
975	
976	    @contextmanager
977	    def _registry_guard(self) -> Iterator[None]:
978	        # WHY: the ONLY override. The `with` guarantees release even if the body
979	        #      raises a DuplicateTopicError.
980	        with self._registry_lock:
981	            yield
982	```

1065	## 8. Key Flow (sequence diagram)
1066	
1067	**Which flow and why:** I'm showing a blocking `poll` that unblocks on a `publish`, because it touches every class and shows the condition-variable wait/notify firing at runtime — the heart of this design.
1068	
1069	```mermaid
1070	sequenceDiagram
1071	    participant C as Consumer (group billing)
1072	    participant B as MessageBroker
1073	    participant T as Topic orders
1074	    participant CV as Condition
1075	    participant P as Producer
1076	
1077	    C->>B: poll(billing, orders)
1078	    B->>T: resolve topic and group
1079	    B->>CV: wait_for(log_len greater than my offset)
1080	    Note over C,CV: log empty so consumer sleeps and releases the lock
1081	    P->>B: publish(orders, o1)
1082	    B->>T: append o1 at offset 0
1083	    B->>CV: notify_all
1084	    Note over C,CV: consumer wakes and re-checks 1 greater than 0 is true
1085	    CV-->>B: predicate true
1086	    B->>T: read log at offset 0
1087	    B->>B: commit billing offset to 1
1088	    B-->>C: return Message o1
1089	```
1090	
1091	> 🎙️ **Walk it arrow by arrow:** "The consumer calls `poll` on the broker. The broker resolves the topic and group, then calls `wait_for` on the topic's condition. The log is empty, so the predicate is false — the consumer sleeps and releases the lock; that's the blocking part. Now the producer calls `publish`. The broker appends `o1` at offset 0 under the same lock, then calls `notify_all` — the interesting moment. The consumer wakes, re-checks its predicate, '1 greater than 0' is true, so it reads `log[0]`, commits its offset to 1, and returns the message. The producer and consumer never touched each other directly — the condition variable on the topic is the entire coordination point."
1092	
1093	**Why the sequence shows the design is good:** Notice the broker coordinates but holds no offset state itself — the offset lives on the group, the log and condition on the topic. The wait/notify is the only synchronization, scoped to one topic, so unrelated topics never block. That's Single Responsibility and per-topic locking working together.
