1097	## 9. Concurrency, Thread-Safety, Edge Cases & Extensibility
1098	
1099	🗣️ **Step 1 — Name the situation precisely:** "There are TWO concurrency stories here, and only one is a 'bug to fix'. The first is the blocking read — that's a *feature*, implemented with a condition variable: `poll` waits on the topic's `Condition`, `publish` notifies it. Because every append and every read-then-commit happens while holding that condition's lock, the hot path is already correct. The second story is the *registry*: two threads calling `create_topic` or `subscribe` at the same instant both pass the existence check and both mutate the dict — a classic check-then-act race that corrupts the registry."
1100	
1101	🗣️ **Step 2 — State the solution:** "For the registry, I add a single `Lock` and wrap the existence-check-and-insert in it, re-checking inside the lock. That's the `_registry_guard` hook — a no-op in the clean core, a real lock in `ThreadSafeMessageBroker`."
1102	
1103	🗣️ **Step 3 — Name the trade-off:** "The registry lock is held for only a couple of dict writes, never across a blocking poll, so it can't throttle throughput. The per-topic condition lock is what serializes the hot path, and it's scoped per topic, so ten topics publish in parallel."
1104	
1105	🗣️ **Step 4 — Distributed case (if asked):** "Across servers, an in-process lock and an in-memory log don't work. This becomes real Kafka: the log is persisted and partitioned across brokers, offsets are committed back to the cluster (`__consumer_offsets`), and 'blocking poll' becomes a long-poll fetch against a partition leader. Exactly-once needs idempotent producers + transactional offset commits."
1106	
1107	**CONCURRENCY VOCABULARY**
1108	
1109	| Term | Plain meaning | Where it appears here |
1110	|---|---|---|
1111	| **Condition variable** | A lock you can sleep on until signalled | `Topic.cond`; `poll` waits, `publish` notifies |
1112	| **wait / notify_all** | Sleep releasing the lock / wake all sleepers | `cv.wait_for(...)` in `poll`, `cv.notify_all()` in `publish` |
1113	| **Spurious wakeup** | A waiter wakes with no real signal | handled by `wait_for`'s predicate re-check |
1114	| **Critical section** | The block that must run atomically | the append in `publish`; the read-then-commit in `poll` |
1115	| **Check-then-act** | Check a condition, act — but state changed between | `create_topic`'s "not in dict, then insert" |
1116	| **Atomic** | All-or-nothing, no half-state observed | read `log[off]` then `off+1`, both under the lock |
1117	| **Mutex / lock** | Token only one thread holds | the `Condition`'s internal lock; the registry `Lock` |
1118	| **Lock granularity** | How much one lock covers | per-topic condition (fine), per-broker registry lock (coarse but tiny) |
1119	| **Pessimistic locking** | Lock before touching state | the registry `Lock` and the condition lock |
1120	| **Optimistic locking** | Write conditionally, retry on conflict | the distributed compare-and-set on offset commit |
1121	| **Deadlock** | Threads each wait on a lock the other holds | avoided: only one lock held at a time, no nesting across topics |
1122	| **Starvation** | A thread never makes progress | not a risk: `notify_all` wakes everyone; short-held locks |
1123	
1124	**CONCURRENCY FOLLOW-UP Q&A**
1125	
1126	> ❓ "Why a condition variable instead of a queue?"
1127	> "A queue would give me blocking too, but a single shared queue can be read by only one consumer per message — it doesn't fan out to multiple groups. I need every group to see every message, so the data structure has to be a shared log with per-group cursors, and the condition variable is how each group blocks on that shared log."
1128	
1129	> ❓ "Why `notify_all` and not `notify`?"
1130	> "`notify` wakes one arbitrary waiter. If two groups are blocked on the same topic and I only wake one, the other sleeps through a message that's at its offset too. `notify_all` wakes everyone; each group re-checks its own offset and only the ones with a new message proceed — the rest go back to sleep. At interview scale the extra wakeups are cheap; if I had thousands of groups I'd shard or use per-group conditions."
1131	
1132	> ❓ "Why `wait_for` and not a bare `wait()`?"
1133	> "`wait_for` takes a predicate and re-evaluates it on every wakeup, so spurious wakeups and notifies meant for OTHER groups don't make this group read a message that isn't there. A bare `wait` would fall through and I'd have to hand-roll the re-check loop — `wait_for` IS that loop done correctly, including the timeout."
1134	
1135	> ❓ "Two threads of the SAME group poll — does one get a duplicate?"
1136	> "No. The read of `log[offset]` and the `offset += 1` commit are both inside the condition lock, so they're one atomic step. One thread reads offset 5 and bumps to 6; the other then reads 6. The group consumes each message exactly once across all its threads — that's the second concurrency test."
1137	
1138	> ❓ "What's the deadlock risk?"
1139	> "Minimal — a thread only ever holds ONE lock at a time. `poll`/`publish` hold a single topic's condition lock and do no I/O while holding it; `create_topic`/`subscribe` hold the registry lock briefly. There's no nested lock acquisition and no fixed cross-topic ordering to violate, so no cycle."
1140	
1141	> ❓ "Pessimistic or optimistic — which and why?"
1142	> "Pessimistic in-process: the critical sections are tiny and contention is low, so a short-held lock beats a retry loop. For the DISTRIBUTED case I'd flip to optimistic — offset commits are a compare-and-set against the cluster, retried on conflict — because you can't hold a local lock across servers."
1143

DISK PUBLISH:
def publish(self, topic_name: str, payload: str) -> Message:
        """Append a message to a topic's log and wake all blocked consumers."""
        # WHY: reject an empty payload at the boundary — a None/empty publish is
        #      almost always a bug, and silently appending it would let blocked
        #      consumers wake on garbage.
        if not payload:
            raise EmptyPayloadError("cannot publish an empty payload")

        topic = self._require_topic(topic_name)
        cv = self._topic_cv(topic)

        # WHY: hold the topic's condition lock for the append. This is the
        #      critical section: compute the offset and append as one atomic
        #      step so two publishers can't both grab the same offset.
        with cv:
            message = Message(
                offset=topic.next_offset(),
                topic=topic.name,
                payload=payload,
                timestamp=self._clock.now(),
            )
            topic.log.append(message)
            # WHY: notify_all (not notify) — several consumer GROUPS may be
            #      blocked on this topic, and every one of them needs to wake to
            #      check whether this new message is at ITS offset.
            cv.notify_all()

        return message
